summary: Parallel review: FAIL (1 critical, 6 warning, 12 suggestion). Compound gate: PASS

findingsList.text: 🟡 `test/extension-system.test.mjs` — No test imports the actual `examples/extensions/log-phases.mjs`. If someone breaks the example extension's return types, no test catches it. Add a smoke test that loads the real file.

🟡 `test/extension-system.test.mjs` — No test loads `examples/extensions/log-phases.mjs` directly; add a smoke test that imports the real example extension and verifies all four hooks return valid output

🟡 `examples/extensions/log-phases.mjs:22` — `executeRun` uses string interpolation inside the shell command (`${safeId}`, `${safeTitle}`) even though values are sanitized; the sanitization only handles single quotes but not other shell metacharacters like `$()` or backticks — use `%s` format specifier in printf and pass values as separate arguments for defense-in-depth

The primary coverage gap is that no automated test loads the **actual** example file — all tests use synthetic extensions. If someone introduces a bug in `log-phases.mjs`, the test suite won't catch it. This is a backlog item (🟡), not a merge blocker.

🟡 `bin/lib/extensions.mjs:239` — `spawnSync("sh", ["-c", desc.command])` forces all extension commands through shell parsing; consider adding an `argv` array alternative so extensions can avoid shell escaping burden. Document this trust boundary in JSDoc.

The previous security eval flagged `log-phases.mjs:22` as 🟡 shell injection. This was a **false positive** — the code correctly uses the `'\''` single-quote escaping pattern inside single-quoted shell arguments, which disables all shell expansion (`$()`, backticks, `$VAR`). I verified with adversarial inputs including `$(whoami)`, `'; rm -rf /`, and nested quote attacks. The prior eval's suggested fix (`JSON.stringify`) would actually be less safe (double-quoted strings allow `$()` expansion in shell).
